review_summary: Transcript is sufficient for diagnostic questions about evaluation bottlenecks, weak correlation between training loss and long-horizon success, simulation-based evaluation challenges, and the motivation for foundation world models.

question_bank.question: Why is real-world evaluation of robot policies described as a major bottleneck?
choices: Because robot policies can be evaluated instantly from the training loss alone
Because it is expensive, slow, noisy, and sensitive to setup details
Because simulation already matches reality perfectly
Because benchmarks for robots are simpler than ImageNet
question_bank.explanation: Real-world testing is burdensome because many small details change outcomes, and each evaluation cycle can take substantial time and effort.

question_bank.question: What problem is highlighted by the weak correlation between policy training loss and real-world success?
choices: One-step prediction metrics may fail to capture long-horizon task quality
Lower loss always guarantees better robot behavior
Training loss is only useful in supervised image classification
The environment becomes deterministic at test time
question_bank.explanation: A policy can look good under the training objective yet still fail over longer horizons, so model selection becomes much harder.

question_bank.question: Why are foundation world models viewed as a promising future direction?
choices: Because robot interaction data should only be used for one-step action cloning
Because the data may contain reusable structure about how actions change the world, not just how to imitate policies
Because they remove the need for benchmarks entirely
Because they are guaranteed to solve sim-to-real transfer
question_bank.explanation: The closing argument is that large action-conditioned datasets encode dynamics and environment structure, so it is wasteful to use them only for direct policy fitting.
